Next is operational integration. Operational integration allows you to set targets that drive the greatest impact and create the most value. Now you do that by identifying best practices to achieve those targets and replicate those results through other people. Tools to assist with operational integration are examining the highest value contributions. Tools to assist with operational integration are by examining the highest value contributors to the business. Establishing accountability standards and then duplicating best practices. Finding the things that work. Creating a system where other people can replicate and duplicate the impact of the contribution from that work. As part of this step, you should interview each employee to learn what's going on well and where the areas of opportunity are in your newly acquired business. Conduct a technology platform review to include the company's customer relationship management system or better known as a CRM. Refresh the marketing and brand strategy that might be decades old and create a hiring careers page to attract the right people. Also, you should identify key performance indicators or better known as KPIs for each department and position. How else will you measure your effectiveness? How will you know if you are focused on the right aspects of your newly acquired business? You'll see in some of the case studies we have that all of the due diligence and pouring over the financials before closing are great. But they're a representation of what's happened historically and they don't tell the current or full story. Only when you start conducting business as a new owner, you'll see all the challenges of growing the business for the first time. And if you don't have key performance indicators to monitor and you don't know what the best practices are and you don't inspire your team and align them with the success, you're going to find yourself in a whole world of hurt. I promise you. So once you get this discipline in place, you know what the key drivers of the business are. You can start measuring your performance, which is crucial to 10xing your new acquisition. The best business leaders consistently use key performance indicators to measure the most important aspect of their operational strategies. This means improved performance over time. And in periods of business or economic crisis, it's a reliable roadmap for stabilizing or even taking advantage of that situation by growing your business. The opportunity for massive and accelerated growth is especially true when you've planned correctly by aligning your KPIs with the business goals and objectives. Many businesses we work with are distracted by vanity metrics without considering whether or not those metrics are even getting you closer to the goal achievement. For instance, if your email marketing is central to your business's sales strategy, then you need to pay close attention to the specific actions of your end users. Having a high open rate sounds promising and it's certainly a metric that has some merit. But if your email recipients aren't clicking links, filling out forms, or taking the actions that will ultimately drive profitability for your business, then having a high open rate doesn't in itself amount to much. In other words, you need to develop a KPI model, including a checklist and a regular cadence of reporting that creates a picture of the general health of your business so that you can reliably measure and track progress. Make changes in areas that are underperforming. Stop expending energy and resources that are adding no value whatsoever to you or the business and avoid big losses when a crisis hits. It is especially important to the long-term health and growth of your business to have short-term and long-term KPI checklists in each area of your company, such as sales, marketing, finance, customer service, and so on. Everyone on your team should understand how their roles and responsibilities contribute to the overall impact of the business. That's why consistency is so important to this process. No matter what issue comes up, you already have a data set that tells you where the business is, where you want it to go, what you did last time to fix it when you had the same thing happen and it points you to all roads leading to the highest profitability possible. This allows your top performers to reside in an organization, expand inside your organization, and bring more people into the organization. This is how your people stay aligned with your long-term goals as you adapt to market fluctuations and market opportunities. We recommend that you create a checklist of about 4-10 KPIs total. Too few, and you're missing out on key performance metrics that could give you a much-needed warning or be the gateway to extreme, massive 10X growth. Too many KPIs and you're giving all your energy into tracking so many things that you can't find the few things that create the most value. So what you want to do is focus on the key performance indicators and we may include some of those in this session, but focus on the key performance indicators that allow you to understand in real time if the business is moving towards your big picture goals or if you're struggling day by day. In a time of business emergency or struggle, your KPI checklist is going to give you calm in the storm and it will help you prioritize your main revenue streams and play to your strengths. Many businesses thought about shutting down that we were managing during COVID, but when we told them to go to their daily KPIs and track what was actually happening in real time versus what they were afraid could happen, many businesses realized their key performance indicators were still strong. So they did not deviate from what they were doing. While their business competitors shut down, they went into expansion because the things they were afraid of never actually happened. Your KPI model should correlate with your industry. For example, if you're a product oriented business, then sales, marketing, manufacturing and shipping are just a few of the areas that you'd want to focus on. If you're a service based business, then your workforce and your cash flow and your new client acquisition rates and things like that are what you want to focus on. In an effort to give you an even clear picture of how KPIs can differ by industry as well as give you some insights into what your own KPIs should look like, we've provided a few sample KPIs for some of the businesses we work with so you can see exactly what we track. Also in the next module with bonus interviews, I suggest listening to Oliver Fernandez who learned through the process of acquiring a flooring company just how important monitoring his own KPIs were after taking ownership of the business. I've seen this process time and time again transform the way that business owners look at their businesses. The sooner you get in front of this as a new business owner, the faster you're going to have success. And more importantly, I've seen strategic KPI tracking transform the way business owners act in regard to their business. The right data at the right time is knowledge and power. It's non-emotional. Data driven knowledge gives you the ability to act or react fast. And this is what you're going to need as a new business owner. Now use this power to keep yourself focused and driving towards your stated goals and objectives.
